food_subgroup: Braised noodle dish with mushrooms and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ee-Fu noodles or braised Yi Mian. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for braised Ee-Fu noodles with mushrooms in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Ee-Fu noodles or braised Yi Mian as a clinical food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of braised Ee-Fu noodles is approximately 400 g, intended as a shared dish for 1–3 people, yielding roughly 130–400 g per individual depending on portion split.

nutrient_source.title: Fried noodles, Chinese style (Mee goreng) — adapted to braised Ee-Fu noodle profile; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo noodle dish entries and USDA SR Legacy egg noodle data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) / USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for braised Ee-Fu noodles with three mushrooms exists. Values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for braised/stir-fried noodle dishes (similar preparation) with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked egg noodles (NDB 20110) and mushroom components. Ee-Fu noodles are pre-fried egg noodles; braising in stock with oyster sauce and mushrooms yields a moderately energy-dense dish. Macros are consistent with comparable Cantonese braised noodle dishes reported in the literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and analogous braised noodle preparations — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised Ee-Fu noodles. Ee-Fu noodles are pre-fried egg noodles that are subsequently braised. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values for egg noodles (cooked) in the range of 35–46 (mean ~40, low GI). Braising in stock with oyster sauce and mushrooms does not substantially alter the GI relative to the noodle base. GI of 40 is adopted as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 86.0) / 100 = 34.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 21.5 × 4 = 86.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and stock
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of braised Ee-Fu noodles due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (≈1680 mg per 400 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or sharing the dish across more diners can help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low glycemic index from egg noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised Ee-Fu noodles have a low glycemic index (~40) due to the egg noodle base, but the glycemic load per full 400 g serving is high (~34.4); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (100–150 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables or lean protein to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with relatively low saturated fat; high sodium is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate total fat (~4.8 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g), which is acceptable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) poses a risk for blood pressure elevation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and choose preparations with reduced soy and oyster sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Ee-Fu Noodles with Three Mushrooms (三菇伊面) is a classic Cantonese noodle dish widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Hong Kong. Ee-Fu noodles (also spelled Yi Mian or Yee Mee) are distinctive pre-fried egg noodles made from wheat flour and eggs; the deep-frying process gives them a golden, slightly crispy texture before braising. During cooking, the noodles are simmered in a savoury braising liquid composed of chicken or vegetable stock, oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic until they absorb the sauce and become soft, silky, and deeply flavoured. The 'three mushrooms' (三菇) typically refers to a combination of shiitake, straw, and oyster mushrooms, which contribute umami depth, earthy aroma, and a tender bite. The finished dish has a glossy, sauce-coated appearance and a rich, savoury-umami flavour profile. Ee-Fu noodles are traditionally associated with longevity and are commonly served at birthday banquets and festive occasions in Chinese culture. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~148 kcal/100 g), moderate carbohydrate (~21.5 g/100 g) with a low glycemic index (~40), modest protein (~5.2 g/100 g), and relatively low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g). The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) from the sauce components, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low GI of the egg noodle base makes this dish more suitable for glycaemic management than high-GI rice-based alternatives, provided portion size is moderated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for braised Ee-Fu noodles with three mushrooms. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Foodo braised/stir-fried noodle dish analogues and USDA SR Legacy egg noodle data. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database egg noodle values. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and cross-referenced across two approved sources, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available.
notes: Serving size of 400 g is estimated based on the restaurant description 'FOR 1-3 pax' and typical Cantonese shared-dish portion norms for a small (小) order. Vitamin B12 is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy analogous entries used for estimation. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy egg noodle data; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain pork-derived oyster sauce or non-halal stock depending on the restaurant; The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon) does not hold a MUIS halal certification as of the data collection date. The cuisine_tags 'BBQ' and 'Crab' from the input source appear to be restaurant-level tags not specific to this dish and have not been carried forward as food-level attributes.
